headline: CORN BREAKS BELOW $4.65 AS PLANTING HITS TARGET
subheadline: Grains fade on fund liquidation while livestock holds mixed ground.
lead: Corn tested $4.65 support and closed $4.63, down 1.9% and the lowest close since April, as funds liquidated grain positions ahead of weekend positioning. The nearby contract pressured the $4.65 level that held all week, with December corn following at $4.87. USDA's afternoon planting report showed corn at 47% complete, above the five-year average of 45%, removing the last weather premium from July contracts.
the_takeaway: Corn planting ahead of schedule removes weather risk premium from futures.
teaser: Funds dump grain positions as corn planting exceeds expectations.

one_number.unit: corn planting progress as of Sunday
one_number.context: USDA's Crop Progress report shows corn planting running two percentage points ahead of the five-year average of 45%. The Belt is officially on schedule with three weeks left in the planting window. Weather premium is gone until something breaks.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called seasonal demand premium building faster than supply could respond in hogs.
yesterdays_call.note: Hogs dropped 1.1% today, seasonal demand story didn't hold against fund rotation.

sections.title: Grains Crack Support
sections.body: Corn lost $4.65 and never looked back, closing $4.63 on the heaviest fund liquidation in three weeks. **December corn broke the 24-cent carry**, falling to $4.87 as prevent-plant anxiety evaporated with today's planting numbers. Soybeans followed corn lower, dropping to $11.84 as China trade talks produced **no concrete purchase commitments** despite White House optimism. The July-November soybean spread compressed another 3 cents as new-crop selling accelerated.
sections.catalyst: USDA Crop Progress shows corn 47% planted, above 5-year average
sections.bottom_line: Weather premium gone, funds rotating out of grains into livestock.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: yesterday's drift became outright breakdown below key support.

sections.title: Livestock Holds Ground
sections.body: Live cattle held $246.40, up a dime, as **JBS reported $221 million Q1 profit** despite U.S. beef margin pressure. The cash market is finding support from packer demand even as feeders eased 0.6% to $358.23. Hogs gave up yesterday's gains, falling 1.1% to $104.78 as funds rotated out of protein and back into equity indexes. Class III milk dropped 1% to $17.13 on seasonal flush timing.
sections.catalyst: JBS Q1 earnings show Brazil operations offsetting U.S. beef headwinds
sections.bottom_line: Cattle acting like the buyer is patient, hogs following fund flows.

sections.title: Energy Slides on Weekend
sections.body: Crude dropped $2.43 to $99.91 as **Hormuz supply fears** took a back seat to weekend positioning. The Iran-Iraq standoff that's dominated energy markets since March showed no new escalation today, allowing long funds to book profits after this week's 7% rally. Natural gas eased to $2.92, still pricing in summer cooling demand but lacking fresh catalysts.
sections.catalyst: No new escalation in Iran-Iraq Hormuz tensions, profit-taking dominates
sections.bottom_line: Geopolitical premium intact but not expanding into weekend.

outside_the_pit.title: H9N2 Bird Flu Strain Shows Greater Infection Potential
outside_the_pit.body: New research shows H9N2 avian influenza demonstrates significantly higher replication rates and spillover potential compared to current strains. The findings matter for poultry market structure even though spot prices aren't reacting yet.

outside_the_pit.title: Fertilizer Supply Still Squeezed by Middle East Conflict
outside_the_pit.body: Fertilizer Institute CEO told Senate Ag Committee that Iran-Iraq tensions continue disrupting global fertilizer production. Tighter supplies are building input cost pressure for 2027 planting season.

outside_the_pit.title: China Expected to Buy Double-Digit Billions in US Farm Goods
outside_the_pit.body: White House expects China to commit to "double-digit billions" in U.S. agricultural purchases following Trump-Xi summit in Beijing. No timeline or commodity breakdown provided, but soybeans likely lead the list.

spread_to_watch.label: Corn July/December spread
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry trade that dominated corn for six weeks is breaking down as planting pressure disappears. When the calendar spread compresses below 20 cents, the old-crop premium story is officially over.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol restart
basis.body: **Eastern Corn Belt basis is tightening** as ethanol plants come back online after spring maintenance. Producers with old-crop storage east of the Mississippi have a window the futures board isn't pricing. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal movement toward export terminals.

weekly_thread.question: Will corn planting hit 45% by Friday to stay on the five-year average?
weekly_thread.status_text: Answered: corn hit 47% planted, two points above the five-year average. The Belt stayed ahead of schedule all week and weather premium is officially gone.

the_more_you_know.title: 47% planted: when weather risk leaves the building
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 47% corn planting figure crosses the threshold where weather anxiety transforms into yield potential. Above 45% planted by mid-May historically removes prevent-plant premiums from July contracts. The market's been pricing 2-3 cents of weather risk since April. That premium just walked out the door. From here, corn needs actual weather problems, not calendar anxiety, to justify risk premiums above the seasonal lows.

watch_list.desc: USDA Export Sales: corn above 400K MT keeps pace with last year
Cattle cash trade: $185 cutout needs to hold for live cattle floor
EIA petroleum status: crude stocks above 450M barrels caps rally
USDA Crop Progress: soybean planting above 25% confirms Belt timing

daily_quote.text: In the midst of chaos, there is also opportunity.

source_summary: USDA Crop Progress, CME futures settlements, JBS earnings, Fertilizer Institute testimony
